story:
I wake up dead again. The sun is too bright, needling through my ruined eyes. Something smells sweet—like meat—and the word slips in and out of reach, the way all words do now. Faces, laughter, a whole life used to live in me; now there is only hunger, always screaming.

I stand on a crooked ankle and drag the bad leg through mud and grass. My body feels borrowed, a costume worn wrong. The others are like me. We don’t talk. We make sounds that are not words while the wind whistles through rib and tooth. I try to taste language, like smoke after a fire, but it won’t stay.

The sweet smell grows stronger. We follow. We find a warm thing that runs on two legs, smoother than I can. Its eyes are wide. Its mouth throws fast noise that might be words. It trips, and the sweetness bursts open.

We fall on it—teeth, bone, clumsy hands. My jaw misses more than it hits. It doesn’t taste like food should, but it quiets the scream for a moment. When the warmth leaves, we stop. I stare at what’s left and tilt my head. The line of a jaw, the curve of a cheekbone—familiar—and then the thought rots and slides away. Empty again.

Night doesn’t bring sleep. We stand in fields of crickets and broken fences, past cars like open mouths and windows staring like blank eyes. I ask myself why I move at all. Is it only hunger? If the hunger stopped, would I? The others won’t answer. Maybe I don’t want them to.

A different thing comes one night, rumbling on wheels with lights and a coughing metal voice. So many warm things inside. Their sticks roar like thunder and some of us fall. I don’t. I move toward the noise. Fire snaps in the dark, something tears through my side, but pain is only a dim pressure now, the space between what I was and what I am.

One of the warm ones sees me. He stops. I stop too. I know his face. The memory strikes like lightning, splitting me open. His name is David. I had a name once. I had a yellow-and-white house, a kitchen that smelled like coffee and banana muffins. It flashes and then blows away like smoke.

“Mom?” he says. His voice breaks. The word goes into me like a blade. I want to answer. I want to tell him I am here, that it is cold inside my head, that I didn’t mean to leave the stove on, that I didn’t mean for any of this. My mouth only makes that rattling sound.

“Mom,” he says again, softer. His stick drops. The others tell him to lift it, to pull, but he doesn’t. Neither do I. We shake across a distance bigger than the sky. I try to step closer. He lifts his hands like he might catch me.

Thunder again—not from him, but from them. Fire through my chest, through my throat, through the place where my name used to be. The world tilts. I fall.

The hunger goes quiet, truly quiet, for the first time since the sky fell and the world burned. I expect freedom. I get cold. Then a hand finds mine—small, warm. David’s hand, like when he was little and crept into my bed after storms. I almost smell his shampoo, hear the whisper of his fear.

The hand squeezes. Maybe it’s a memory. Maybe it’s him. For a moment I am not a thing that walks and rots and hungers. I am only a mother.

Then the dark takes me.
